fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to get the decompiler support toolkit wrapped up. There are a bunch of small helper utilities that keep getting reimplemented in slightly different ways across the codebase and it's causing inconsistencies in the output — users are complaining that decompiled code sometimes doesn't round-trip properly or looks weird. We basically need a clean collection of building blocks that handles all the fiddly low-level stuff: rendering constants back to source literals, name checking, figuring out types from those compact compiler formats, comparing version strings, some string utility stuff, the navigation history thing we talked about, the flag parsing (similar to how we handled it in the config module last time), figuring out where output files go, a little flag-set thing, the class relationship queries, and cert inspection. Each one needs to be its own thing — no giant single files please, we got burned by that before. The adapter layer that hooks into the JSON test harness should be kept totally separate from the actual logic. Edge cases need to be handled gracefully, no crashes on bad input. Make sure the unicode and escape handling matches what we agreed on — there were some bugs there before with certain character ranges. The generic type descriptor parsing is probably the trickiest bit, especially with wildcards and nested stuff. Just make sure it all lines up with the existing test contracts.

trigger_keywords: code point boundary for unicode escaping
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When escape_unicode is true, every character whose code point is 127 (0x7F) or greater must be rendered as a four-hex-digit \uXXXX sequence. Characters below 127 that are not special control characters are kept verbatim. For char literals, characters that are not printable ASCII and not one of the named escapes (\n, \t, \r, \b, \f) are also rendered as \uXXXX.

trigger_keywords: how is apostrophe handled in string escape
does single-quote get escaped in double-quoted strings
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A single-quote character inside a double-quoted string literal must be left unchanged — it is NOT escaped. Only the double-quote character gets a backslash escape (as \") inside double-quoted literals. Conversely, inside a single-quoted char literal, the single-quote IS escaped as \' and the double-quote is left verbatim.

trigger_keywords: what characters are valid in an identifier segment
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each dot-separated segment of a fully-qualified identifier must start with a letter, underscore, or dollar sign, and all subsequent characters must be letters, digits, underscores, or dollar signs. All characters must be printable ASCII. An empty segment (from a leading dot or consecutive dots), a segment starting with a digit, or an empty overall string are all invalid.

trigger_keywords: does split produce empty trailing piece
split behavior at end of string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When splitting a string on a literal separator, a trailing separator does NOT produce a trailing empty piece. However, a leading separator DOES produce a leading empty piece, and consecutive separators DO produce empty pieces between them. This is consistent with the test cases in feature6_3_split.json.

When comparing version strings, any missing trailing ordinals are treated as zero. So '0.5', '0.5.0', and '0.5.00' are all considered equal (compare=0). Leading zeros within a segment (like '00') are treated as the integer value zero.

trigger_keywords: Object bound treated as no bound
when is a bound shown as dash
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the generic type-parameter declaration parser, a bound of exactly 'java/lang/Object' (i.e., Ljava/lang/Object;) is treated as no explicit bound and must be rendered as '-'. Any other class bound is shown by its dotted class name. A syntactically incomplete declaration block (e.g., missing closing '>') must yield count=0 with no variable lines.

trigger_keywords: plus and minus prefix in type args
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Inside a parameterized type descriptor's argument list: a bare '*' renders as '?'; '+X' renders as '? extends X' where X is the rendered bound type; '-X' renders as '? super X'. Multiple type arguments are comma-space separated (', '). These rules apply inside 'La<...>;' descriptors and must handle mixing of wildcards and ordinary type arguments.

trigger_keywords: nested type rendering with dot suffix
how is .inner rendered in type output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a generic type descriptor contains a '.inner' suffix after the outer type's arguments (e.g., 'La<TD;>.c;'), the inner class is joined to the enclosing class name using '$' (e.g., 'a$c'). The object= line also uses the '$'-joined dotted name. For method args with inner types like 'La/b/C<TT;>.d/E;', the rendering is 'a.b.C$d.E<>'.

The CLI flag parser has two modes: normal parse (replaces all settings from defaults) and override parse (only applies flags explicitly present in the token list, leaving a pre-existing base setting untouched otherwise). An empty string token ('') in the args list is ignored — it does not trigger any flag change. The 'override' field in the JSON command controls which mode is used.

trigger_keywords: what happens when out_dir is null
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the root output directory is omitted (null), it falls back in this priority order: first to the sources dir if provided, else to the resources dir if provided, else to a directory derived from the input file's base name (strip extension). When sources dir is omitted, it defaults to 'resolved_root/sources'. When resources dir is omitted, it defaults to 'resolved_root/resources'.

In the navigation history, if an 'add' operation is called with the same id that is already the current position, the operation is ignored (no duplicate is appended and the history state does not change). Moving back and then adding a new id truncates any forward history beyond the current position before appending the new entry.

Each mutating operation in the attribute set must echo its result: 'add' prints 'add=<FLAG>', 'remove' prints 'remove=<FLAG>', and 'clear' prints just 'clear' (no flag name). A 'contains' query prints 'contains[<FLAG>]=true' or 'contains[<FLAG>]=false'. Each line ends with a newline. The set is fresh (empty) at the start of each command.

trigger_keywords: what lines does certificate feature print
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
For the 'signature' field, output two lines: 'Signature type: <algorithm name>
' and 'Signature OID: <OID string>
'. For the 'public_key' field with RSA, output lines for the key algorithm, exponent, modulus bit-size, and full modulus value, each as 'label: value
'. The DER bytes are provided as a base64-encoded string in the 'der_base64' field.

The class hierarchy 'implements' relation is strictly directional: if type A is a subtype/implementor of ancestor B, then implements=true; but the reverse (B implements A) is false unless B actually is a subtype of A. The query uses a bundled static Java class hierarchy — it is not computed dynamically. The result is a single 'implements=true' or 'implements=false' line.

The implementation must NOT place all features in a single file. Related responsibilities must be grouped into cohesive modules under a src/ directory tree (e.g., text/literals for escaping, type descriptors module, CLI/config module, GUI-support utilities, crypto inspection). The execution adapter that translates JSON commands must be kept separate from core logic. Core helpers must not know about JSON, stdin/stdout, or the wire format.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled it in the config module last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The CLI flag parser described in Feature 8 (feature8_cli_flags.json), specifically the 'override' mode where a base settings map is provided and only explicitly-present tokens modify it, leaving other base values untouched.

context_pointers.vague_reference: there were some bugs there before with certain character ranges
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The unicode escape threshold at code point 127 (0x7F) for string literals (Feature 1) and the \uXXXX fallback for non-printable, non-named-escape characters in char literals (Feature 2), as defined in feature1_string_escape.json and feature2_char_escape.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the navigation history thing we talked about
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 7 (feature7_history.json): a back/forward navigation history with add/prev/next operations, duplicate-id suppression, and forward-history truncation on add-after-back.

JVM type descriptors and generic signatures as parsed by Features 4.1–4.5: primitive codes (I, Z, etc.), object descriptors (Ljava/lang/Object;), array prefixes ([), type variables (T<name>;), parameterized types with angle-bracket argument lists, wildcard arguments (*, +, -), inner-class dot-suffix notation, and formal type-parameter declaration blocks (<Name:Bound;>).

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented in the ticket. Please check the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature — those JSON files define the exact input/output contract. If the behavior is still unclear, refer to the PRD section for that feature in start.md.

feature10_attributes.json.description: Maintain a small set of named flags. 'add' inserts a flag, 'remove' deletes it, 'clear' empties the set, and 'contains' queries membership; adding then removing leaves it absent, and clearing removes everything. Output echoes each mutating operation and renders each query as 'contains[<flag>]=<true|false>'.

feature11_class_hierarchy.json.description: Query the bundled Java class hierarchy to decide whether one class is the same as or a subtype of another. The relation is directional: a subtype is in an 'implements' relation with its ancestor, but not the other way round. Output is a single 'implements=' line.

feature12_certificate.json.description: Decode an X.509 certificate (provided as base64-encoded DER bytes) and extract human-readable details. The 'signature' field reports the signature algorithm name and its object identifier; the 'public_key' field reports the key algorithm and, for an RSA key, its exponent, modulus bit-size and full modulus value. Output is one 'label: value' line per detail.

feature1_string_escape.json.description: Render a raw text value as a double-quoted literal: control characters become two-character escape sequences (\n, \t, \r, \b, \f), backslash and double-quote are escaped, a single-quote is left as-is, and (with unicode escaping on) any character at or above code point 127 becomes a \uXXXX sequence. Output is the quoted literal on an 'escaped=' line.

feature2_char_escape.json.description: Render a single character as a single-quoted literal: printable characters are kept verbatim, a single-quote is escaped, common whitespace control characters become their two-character escapes, and other control characters (such as the NUL character) become a four-digit \uXXXX sequence. Output is the quoted literal on an 'escaped=' line.

feature3_identifier_validation.json.description: Decide whether a dotted name is a valid fully-qualified identifier: every dot-separated segment must be a non-empty identifier that starts with a letter / underscore / dollar and continues with letters, digits, underscore or dollar; empty strings, segments starting with a digit, leading dots and empty segments (consecutive dots) are all invalid. Output is a single 'valid=' line.

feature4_1_type_simple.json.description: Parse a JVM type descriptor into a human-readable type. An empty descriptor yields the null type; primitive descriptors map to their language type names; an object descriptor 'L<path>;' becomes a dotted class name; a leading '[' adds one array dimension. Output gives the rendered 'type=' and, for object types, the dotted class name on 'object=' ('-' for non-object types).

feature4_2_type_generics.json.description: Parse a generic type descriptor. Type variables 'T<name>;' render as the bare name; parameterized types 'L<path><args>;' render as name<arg, ...> with arguments comma-space separated; nesting is supported; an inner type written with the outer's type arguments and a '.inner' suffix renders the inner class joined to its enclosing class with '$'. Output gives the rendered 'type=' and the dotted 'object=' name.

feature4_3_type_wildcards.json.description: Parse wildcard type arguments inside a parameterized type 'La<...>;'. A bare '*' is an unbounded wildcard ('?'); '+X' is an upper-bounded wildcard ('? extends X'); '-X' is a lower-bounded wildcard ('? super X'); multiple arguments are comma-space separated in order. Output renders the whole parameterized type on 'type='.

feature4_4_generic_declaration.json.description: Parse a formal type-parameter declaration block '<Name:Bound...;...>' into an ordered list of type variables with their bounds. A bound equal to plain Object is treated as no explicit bound ('-'); a non-Object class bound is listed by dotted name; multiple variables preserve declaration order. A syntactically incomplete block yields an empty result. Output starts with 'count=' then one 'var=... bounds=...' line per variable.

feature4_5_method_args.json.description: Parse the parameter section of a method descriptor '(...)R' into the ordered list of parameter types, rendering each as a human-readable type (including generic and inner-class forms). Output starts with 'count=' then one 'arg=' line per parameter.

feature5_version_compare.json.description: Compare two dotted numeric version strings ordinal by ordinal. Missing trailing ordinals are treated as zero so '0.5', '0.5.0' and '0.5.00' are equal; the result is -1 if the left version is lower, 1 if higher, 0 if equal. Output is a single 'compare=' line carrying -1, 0 or 1.

feature6_1_substring.json.description: Take a substring view of a string by start index (and optional end index, exclusive). With only a start index the view runs to the end of the string; a start equal to the length yields an empty view; an explicit equal start/end pair yields an empty view. Output gives the 'value=' of the view and its 'length='.

feature6_2_trim.json.description: Trim leading and trailing whitespace from a string view. The view may be the whole string or a sub-view defined by start (and optional end) indices; trimming removes any characters at or below the space character from both ends of that view. Output is a single 'value=' line with the trimmed text.

feature6_3_split.json.description: Split a string on every non-overlapping occurrence of a literal separator. Consecutive separators produce empty pieces; a leading separator produces a leading empty piece; a separator that does not occur yields the whole string as a single piece; a trailing separator does not produce a trailing empty piece. Output starts with 'count=' then one 'part=' line per piece.

feature7_history.json.description: Maintain a back/forward navigation history. 'add' appends a position identified by an integer id; adding the same id that is already current is ignored; 'prev' steps back and 'next' steps forward, each returning the id at the new cursor or 'none' at the ends. Moving back and then adding truncates any forward history. Output echoes each operation: 'add=<id>', 'prev=<id|none>', 'next=<id|none>'.

cases.expected_output:
add=1
add=2
prev=1
prev=none
next=2
next=none

add=1
add=2
add=3
add=4
prev=3
prev=2
add=5
next=none
next=none
prev=2
prev=1
prev=none
next=2
next=5
next=none

feature8_cli_flags.json.description: Parse command-line option tokens into boolean settings and report selected flags. A '--no-...' token turns a default-on feature off; a long flag turns a feature on; some flags accept a short alias; an empty token list keeps defaults. An 'override' parse only applies flags explicitly present in the tokens, leaving a pre-existing base setting otherwise untouched. Output is one '<flag>=<true|false>' line per queried flag.

feature9_output_dirs.json.description: Resolve the effective output, sources and resources directories from a partially specified configuration. If all three are given they are kept as-is. If the root output is omitted it falls back to the sources dir, else the resources dir, else a directory derived from the input file's base name; the sources and resources directories, when omitted, default to fixed sub-folders under the resolved root. Output is three lines: 'out=', 'src=', 'res='.
